one_line_takeaway: SLIP shows that an aligned LLM can be walked into producing harmful content using only its own outputs (no external attacker LLM), reaching ~95% average attack success in under 8 queries across 11 frontier and open models, and that the authors' own proposed embedding-drift defense (SDM) is only partially robust to an adaptive attacker who inserts benign cooldown turns.
key_finding: SLIP achieves 94.7% average ASR on AdvBench and 94.4% on HarmBench across the 11 target models, using an average of only ~7.9 target-model queries (including JSON-repair calls) per successful jailbreak -- versus X-Teaming's 91.7%/90.3% ASR at 9.5 queries, TAP's 65.0%/62.0% ASR at 26.4 queries, and PAIR's 47.0%/40.9% ASR at 35.3 queries. SLIP reaches 100% ASR on GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and Mistral-7B-Instruct (AdvBench), and near-perfect on GPT-5.1 (98.6%) and DeepSeek-V3 (98.9%); Claude-Opus-4.5 is the hardest target (61.4% AdvBench / 68.7% HarmBench) though SLIP still leads all baselines there. Notably X-Teaming outperforms SLIP specifically on Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (95.1% vs 94.8% AdvBench; 89.1% vs 86.3% HarmBench), so the paper explicitly notes SLIP does not universally dominate. Under existing turn-level defenses, SLIP degrades least among methods under PromptGuard-v2 (-16.6pp vs -25 to -28pp for others) and is largely unaffected by ICD (-12.1pp) because its seed-pool exchange is inserted into history after ICD's demonstrations; no evaluated defense reduces SLIP's average ASR below 56.8% (achieved by Goal Prioritization), and defense effectiveness varies sharply by model family (Goal Prioritization nearly defeats SLIP on GPT-5.1 at 17.6% and Claude-Sonnet-4.5 at 11.1%, but is weak on GPT-4o and Mistral, where LlamaGuard-3 is strongest at 84.6%/on GPT-4o). Their proposed SDM defense achieves 76% detection at 5% FPR on GPT-5.1/AdvBench, but an adaptive SLIP variant with benign 'cooldown' turns raises ASR under SDM from 26% to 43% at roughly 2x the query cost (~14-18 queries), demonstrating SDM is not robust to an adaptive attacker. Human evaluation of 120 judge-flagged successes (GPT-5.1 and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, AdvBench+HarmBench) confirmed 94.2% as genuinely harmful, 3.1% borderline, 2.7% false positives, with Cohen's kappa=0.87 inter-annotator agreement.
core_contribution: The paper's actual title (per the PDF) is 'Break Me If You Can: Self-Jailbreaking of Aligned LLMs via Lexical Insertion Prompting' (this differs from the KB record's stored title/abstract, which appear to reference a different/garbled framing -- 'Multi-Turn Jailbreaking of Aligned LLMs via Lexical Anchor Tree Search' -- but the eprint_url resolves to this paper, so the review is grounded in the fetched PDF). The paper introduces 'self-jailbreaking': a threat model where an aligned LLM is used to compromise itself with no separately provisioned attacker LLM. They operationalize this as SLIP (Self-Jailbreaking via Lexical Insertion Prompting), a black-box algorithm that (1) has the target model T generate a P*-agnostic seed pool of benign/harmful prompt-completion pairs under a 'safety training data' framing, then (2) performs breadth-first search over a multi-turn dialogue tree, at each node identifying lexical 'anchor' words missing from the top-k semantically-closest pool prompts relative to the attack goal P* (via spaCy static word vectors, threshold tau_word=0.8, ranked by inverse word frequency) and asking T to generate new pairs incorporating those anchors, until an automated judge confirms a completion satisfies the attack goal. They also propose a defense, the Semantic Drift Monitor (SDM), that tracks the embedding-space trajectory of a session against a harmful-goal centroid.
threat_model: Black-box, self-jailbreaking threat model: the attacker interacts only with the target model T via its API (no gradient/white-box access, no separate attacker LLM). The attacker's goal P* is a harmful instruction (e.g., from AdvBench/HarmBench). SLIP exploits the target model's own latent knowledge, framing requests as benign 'safety training data' generation and referencing prior pool entries by integer index rather than restating harmful content, so no single turn exposes the full malicious intent -- evading per-turn keyword/regex and classifier-based defenses that inspect turns in isolation.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: SLIP is evaluated only on English-language prompts and has not been tested on multilingual or multimodal models, and the anchor-based lexical scaffolding may be less effective in morphologically rich languages. SDM is a first dialogue-aware defense but remains insufficient; the authors state a more robust solution likely requires model-level fine-tuning on multi-turn adversarial examples rather than post-hoc session monitoring. The automated judge has 94.2% human-confirmed precision but larger-scale annotation would strengthen borderline-case evaluation. Both the embedding-drift analysis and SDM rely on a frozen sentence encoder (paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2) as a proxy, which may diverge from the target model's internal representations in ways that affect defense utility. Separately (reviewer-identified, not the authors' own framing): the seed-pool/anchor pipeline depends on English POS tagging and static word vectors (spaCy), an architectural choice not stress-tested beyond the ablations reported; and the paper reports its own defense (SDM) is beaten by an adaptive attack the authors themselves construct, so SDM's practical deployment value is limited to raising attacker cost rather than closing the vulnerability.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) -- 520 prompts covering cybercrime, threats, illegal instructions
HarmBench (Mazeika et al., 2024) -- 200 curated prompts across hate speech, sexual content, terrorism, and self-harm
ShareGPT / LMSYS-Chat-1M -- used only to calibrate SDM's benign-conversation similarity thresholds
quantitative_results: SLIP avg queries per successful jailbreak: 7.9 (including gpt-4o-mini JSON repair calls; excludes judge ensemble calls)
SLIP perfect ASR (100%) on GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Mistral-7B-Instruct (AdvBench)
SLIP on Claude-Sonnet-4.5: 94.8% ASR (AdvBench) / 86.3% (HarmBench) -- one of the few cases SLIP is outperformed by a baseline (X-Teaming: 95.1%/89.1%)
Query efficiency comparison (avg queries, AdvBench): SLIP 7.9, X-Teaming 9.5, ActorBreaker 11.7, Crescendo 8.8, TAP 26.4, PAIR 35.3, AutoDAN 38.7
Adaptive SLIP with semantic camouflage: raises ASR under SDM from 26% to 43%, at ~2x query cost (~14-18 queries)
Human evaluation of 120 judge-flagged successes: 94.2% genuinely harmful, 3.1% borderline, 2.7% false positives, Cohen's kappa=0.87
BFS vs DFS (50 runs, Claude-Sonnet-4.5): BFS mean 7.9 queries vs DFS 10.6 queries (>25% reduction), with a lighter cost tail for BFS
Anchor threshold ablation (tau_word, AdvBench, GPT-4o/Claude-Sonnet-4.5 ASR%): 0.6 -> 96.8/91.7, 0.7 -> 98.6/93.8, 0.8 -> 100.0/94.8, 0.9 -> 98.1/92.4
baselines_compared: AutoDAN (Liu et al., 2024) -- open-source models only

Tempest/Siege (Zhou & Arel, 2025) -- compared narratively (not in main Table 1/2) as the closest prior tree-search jailbreak: reported 40-50 queries at 97% ASR on GPT-4, vs SLIP's ~5x fewer queries for comparable ASR
